summary: McKenzie reports that his Bingo Card Creator software was cracked by a Chinese warez group, who even translated some of his marketing materials into simplified Chinese. While he acknowledges this led to his highest traffic day ever, the traffic is worthless since it comes from piracy seekers. He considers retaliatory redirects but lacks the technical means on his Windows hosting. The post captures the early frustrations of a solo software developer dealing with software piracy for the first time.
key_points: His software was cracked and redistributed by a Chinese warez group shortly after launch
The piracy ironically generated his highest traffic day, though the traffic had no commercial value
He lacked the server configuration knowledge on Windows hosting to implement countermeasures
He treated the situation with humor rather than panic, suggesting it wouldn't meaningfully impact his business
money_quotes: My software got cracked yesterday by a Chinese warez group. They even went to the trouble of translating some of my marketing material into simplified Chinese — thanks guys, I think…
Its really annoying to have my highest traffic day ever as a result of a steady stream of traffic from their website.
key_insight: Early-stage software piracy is more of an annoyance than an existential threat for a micro-ISV, and vanity metrics like traffic spikes are meaningless when driven by the wrong audience.
